data.title: Lazard International Quality Growth Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

  • Driven by Europe, and helped by stronger currencies, international equities rose strongly.
  • International equities outperformed equities in the United States by the largest quarterly amount in more than two decades.
  • European defense spending and potential German fiscal spending, both significant, contributed to strong European returns.
  • Concerns over tariffs contributed to higher inflation expectations. Amidst higher rates, Value significantly outperformed as banks extended their run within financials, followed by other outperforming sectors like energy and utilities. Quality significantly lagged the market during the last two quarters.

Led by European corporate fundamentals, increasing optimism around fiscal stimulus, and strengthening currencies, international equities staged the largest quarterly outperformance over US equities in over two decades.

Valuations have been inexpensive for international equities for several years. International equities experienced two large changes this year that could change that discount. The first was DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), a Chinese artificial intelligence (AI) company, announcing in January that it could offer open-source, high-performance AI models at a fraction of the cost of traditional AI giants. This caused competitors to pause and re-evaluate how much they spend on training AI models, and investors to rethink the valuations they are willing to pay for AI-related companies. While the concentration of the US market remains significantly tilted to many of these AI- related companies, whose stretched valuations are dependent on continued significant investment spend, DeepSeek has called some of that into question. As a result, global investors began to shift money from the expensive US market to the less expensive international markets.

Soon after the DeepSeek competitive revaluation, economic forecasts began to predict that global GDP could be negatively affected, and US tariffs could further impact the longer-term inflation outlook.

The tectonic shift in geopolitics continued as the US appeared to step back from its role of providing defense support for European allies. At the same time, Friedrich Merz, winner of the German election, pledged to focus on European cooperation. His spending and investment plan for Germany stands in stark contrast to the historic fiscal conservatism of German governments. The relatively low level of German public debt has enabled Merz to pivot and potentially provide the much-needed fiscal stimulus to Europe’s largest economy. The potential for a multi-year increase in fiscal stimulus in Germany, targeted at transport, communication, digital and power infrastructure, coupled with a significantly higher level of targeted defense spending across Europe, gave some investors hope that this is the fiscal unification moment that Europe has needed.

Geopolitical volatility and the shift in relative growth rates between the US and Europe have also moved bond markets and raised expectations for higher future rates.

Relatively higher bond yields (the discount rate for equities), coupled with lower expectations for growth in traditional Growth sectors such as technology and consumer discretionary, have driven a significant move toward Value, where the investment horizon is usually shorter, and away from Quality, where the investment horizon is usually longer. Within financials, the banking industry continued its extraordinary performance, leading the All Country World ex-US Index by 6% during the quarter and 17% over the past year. Other traditional Value sectors, such as energy and utilities, also outperformed during the quarter.

In this shifting environment of higher rates and slower growth expectations, Quality companies lagged Value by a wide margin. While the fundamental earnings power of certain quality companies we own continues to rise more than the market, the derating of the valuation has been significant. Over the past six months, international quality investors have seen two of the worst four quarters for Quality since 2001.

Portfolio Review

During the first quarter, the Lazard International Quality Growth Portfolio rose 1.9% (net of fees1), lagging the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index and the MSCI All Country World ex-US Growth Index, which rose 5.2% and 2.0%, respectively. Our investment philosophy is to seek the highest quality companies. We define these companies as those we believe can generate the highest levels of financial productivity with enduring competitive advantages that will enable them to continue delivering that high financial productivity well into the future and those that can reinvest for growth.

We expect the Portfolio to perform well in most market environments, driven by the consistency with which Quality has tended to perform as a style over many years. When economies are growing, we would expect performance to be driven by the compounding of cash flows as the companies we hold reinvest to drive growth. In recessionary environments, we would expect the high-quality characteristics, coupled with the barriers to competition the companies possess, to allow them to maintain pricing power and see smaller declines in earnings.

When compared to the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index, we expect to participate, but potentially lag in short-term, low-quality led market environments. When compared to the MSCI All Country World ex-US Growth Index, we expect to participate, but potentially lag a market led significantly by unprofitable momentum growth companies, which are most often associated with excessively low interest rates environments.

The past two quarters were two of the strongest quarters favoring the Value style that we have seen in decades.

During the first quarter, our Portfolio companies experienced positive EPS revisions that were stronger than the market. What hurt performance was the derating of the valuation of the companies compared to the market. When looking at each stock in the Portfolio, roughly two-thirds saw their forward EPS estimates rise more than the market. However, nearly the same number saw valuations fall more than the market.

Net-of-fees performance reflects the deduction of the highest tier investment management fee charged based on Lazard’s stand institutional separate account fee schedule for the Lazard International Quality Growth strategy, which is 0.75% per annum.

Positives

Stock selection in the information technology sector contributed positively to relative returns.

  • Based in Israel, Check Point Software Technologies (CHKP) (2.8% weighting in the Portfolio) is a leading global, supplier of cybersecurity products and services to both governments and corporations. Shares rose after the company reported fourth quarter and full-year results. Gross margin came in at 89% for both the quarter and the full-year, while net margin came in at 43% and 41%, respectively, for the quarter and the year, highlighting industry-leading profitability. Total billings (revenue) grew 11% year-over- year, and RPO (remaining performance obligation), which reflects non-cancelable products or services that are yet to be reflected as revenue, grew 12% year-over-year. These results, which highlighted industry-leading profitability, are supportive of our investment thesis.
  • TOTVS (OTCPK:TTVSY) (1.6% weighting in the Portfolio) is the dominant provider of ERP (enterprise resource planning) solutions in Brazil and is expanding into CRM (customer relationship management) solutions. Brazil has a complicated tax regulation, which creates a high entry barrier for foreign ERP software companies and helps TOTVS maintain its dominant place in the market. TOTVS’ has approximately a 97% retention rate, and annualized recurring revenue accounts for more than 90% of its total revenue, creating a solid base to grow from. Shares rose after the company reported consensus-beating fourth-quarter results, where strong group performance was supported by broad-based segment performance and recurring revenue growth supported margin expansion.

Stock specific contributors to relative returns included:

  • Scout24 (OTCPK:SCOTF) (2.0% weighting in the Portfolio), a German holding company for internet businesses with subsidiaries in finance, accounting, controlling, internal audit, risk management, compliance, strategy, etc. The ImmoScout24 is a digital marketplace for residential and commercial real estate. Shares rose after the company’s fourth quarter results highlighted strong performance across all businesses. Management guided for 12-14% revenue growth in 2025, ahead of consensus estimates, and commented on improving macro conditions. They expect an improvement in transactions in the property market to translate to greater demand for the company’s core agent subscription products (e.g., recurring charges for premium packages with better listing placement) and pay per add (PPA) spend (charged for each listing). We trimmed the portfolio’s position as a source of funds for InterContinental Hotels Group.

Negatives

Close to half the underperformance during the quarter came from allocation, providing more evidence that the Value style was dominant this quarter. In fact, the combination of our underweight to banks and our overweights to commercial services (an industry comprised of many high quality, asset-light businesses) and technology cost roughly 70% of the total underperformance in the quarter relative to the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index.

There were some stock specific detractors from performance.

  • Companies with higher US exposure:

Domiciled in Ireland, Accenture (ACN) (2.7% weighting in the Portfolio) is one of the highest-quality IT services companies benefiting from the global transformation of digital infrastructure. While recent results came in slightly ahead of consensus expectations, shares underperformed on macroeconomic uncertainty and softer discretionary spending conditions. Despite this, Accenture’s trailing 12-month AI bookings remain 15-20% above revenue run rates, and the backlog continues to grow. In the long term, we expect Accenture to continue to lead customers through the digital disruption taking place in all industries, and this differentiation coupled with the company’s unique vertical capabilities should enable strong growth and market share gains.

Based in Japan, Recruit (OTCPK:RCRRF) (1.8% weighting in the Portfolio) owns the leading online job recruiting platforms, including Indeed, Glassdoor, and RFG Staffing. In Japan, it is the leader in online listing/matching solutions for staffing, real estate, beauty, and bridal, as well as a host of services. The company has maintained pricing power despite a reduction in job openings, demonstrating strong barriers to competition, which we think will allow returns to be sustained. Recruit implemented predictive supply-demand models to optimize job posting prices based on location and job type, and is also leveraging AI to automate job searches and improve internal productivity. It has a large job posting and candidate data, which it feeds into its algorithm to refine its search models. Recruit has placed focus on increasing the take rates (commission) by shifting more customers from freemium to paying models. To do so, Recruit continues to demonstrate a clear return on investment (achieved through case studies, testimonials, and data-driven insights) to its customers. Despite reporting third quarter results that came in slightly ahead of consensus expectations, shares underperformed because of investors’ concerns over a lower number of job openings. We think the share price reaction is overdone and not reflective of the fundamentals of the company. Recruit generates nearly a 20%, and improving, ROE with a net cash balance sheet and, we think, it is well-positioned for ample growth.

  • Spirits company:

Based in the United Kingdom, Diageo (DEO) (1.8% weighting in the Portfolio) is the leader in the international spirits’ industry. It has leading positions in the most attractive spirits categories (tequila and whiskey) and exposure to high-growth markets like India (where it has the number one market share). We think Diageo is poised to benefit from continued strong growth of the spirits’ industry, and it has improved its portfolio of brands and its premium weight, allowing it to potentially outperform the industry growth rate consistently. The moat of the business is wide, with two-thirds of sales in aged liquid that requires significant strategic inventories and infrastructure investments. Despite reporting first-half results that saw organic revenue growth beat consensus expectations, the timing of Diageo’s reporting coincided with tariff news. As a result, management felt it was not appropriate to provide a new medium-term guidance target at the time of the release, causing the shares to underperform. We think the impact of tariffs is already priced into the shares, and continue to see favorable upside for Diageo. The industry has an attractive growth outlook (mid-single-digit growth) driven by product mix in developed markets and increasing per capita consumption in emerging markets. Margins and returns are high, with a wide moat, especially for companies that primarily sell aged spirits (such as Diageo). Diageo continues to gain market share and improve free cash flow, and we think it can continue to do so going forward.

Recent Activity and Trade Rationale

Buys

  • InterContinental Hotels Group (IHG) (1.7% weighting in the Portfolio), is the fourth-largest hotel group globally by rooms and system size and one of the few global asset-light hotel models based in the United Kingdom.
  • Japan-based Recruit (1.8% weighting in the Portfolio), which owns the leading online job recruiting platforms, including Indeed, Glassdoor, and RFG Staffing.

Sells

  • Based in Canada, BRP (DOOO) (0.0% weighting in the Portfolio) is the leading power sports OEM (original equipment manufacturer) globally. We exited our position as weaker than expected barriers to competition broke our investment thesis.
  • Based in Japan, Shimano (OTCPK:SHMDF) (0.0% weighting in the Portfolio) is a leading high-end bicycle components manufacturer. We exited our position as a source of funds for Recruit.

Outlook

  • Heightened geopolitical uncertainty is likely to persist, and may result in slower investment and consumption.
  • As slower growth and higher inflation fears increase, we expect quality companies to be well-positioned to resume leadership.
  • The historical magnitude of the underperformance of quality suggests the style may outperform going forward.
  • The drivers of US outperformance are diminishing or reversing.
  • European fiscal stimulus plans could provide continued tailwind for European equities.
  • International valuations remain attractive compared to those of the US.

Tariffs and defense spending were major points of discussion in the first quarter. Company managements have attempted to determine the impact of tariffs on their businesses. From sales demand to pricing to margins, tariffs and defense spending affect many facets of investment planning and decision-making. In the near term, the likely outcome is that management teams will remain cautious and wait to see how the situation sorts out. In the meantime, sales and profits could be negatively impacted. We have been speaking with companies in our Portfolio, trying to assess how these new policies will impact their businesses.

One likely outcome of these new policies is slower growth and rising inflation. In this environment, investors initially reached for precious metals as a real asset store of value, and we have seen gold and silver rally substantially in the first quarter. Banks also rallied significantly, as they are also viewed as beneficiaries as interest rates rise, increasing their net interest margins, and investors believe loan demand will remain robust as businesses seek to capitalize on the inflationary environment.

What happens from here? We believe that companies with stronger pricing power will more likely be able to pass along higher costs to their customers without seeing demand deteriorate and will likely have a higher probability of maintaining their margins.

Companies with the widest moats, the highest financial productivity, and the opportunity to reinvest for growth are likely to resume leadership. During the past six months, Quality businesses derated and underperformed by more than 5%. In the past quarter century, every time quality underperformed by that magnitude or more, it outperformed in the subsequent period. We believe companies that can organically grow earnings while maintaining margins and profitability will be leaders going forward, and, therefore, we expect history to repeat.

data.title: Merck: Defensive Yield Meets Long-Term Optionality
data.description:
Merck & Co. (NYSE:MRK) is a defensive play with good dividend prospects and long-term optionality, making it a patient GARP style buy. Investors should not expect immediate excitement barring term technical bounce backs. I position Merck as a stable compounder with low speculative biotech risks - a core portfolio stabilizer in a larger portfolio and certainly in your biotech portfolio.

The Keytruda Question

Concentration risk is high on Keytruda. Close to 45% of all revenue came from the cancer drug in Q1 2025. The Keytruda Loss of Exclusivity (LOE) is slated to start in 2028, leading to biosimilars eroding the market share. Although biologics like Keytruda will likely see slower erosion than small molecule drugs, a significant revenue gap is evident.

In 2024, total Keytruda sales were $29.5b. I have modeled the impact aggressively to study whether valuations and prices reflect the cliff effect. I have assumed that the LOE will cause a sharp decline in revenue over the first three years after patent expiration, i.e., revenue drops to approximately 50% in year 1, 25% in year 2, and 15% in year 3. Additional revenue erosion is considered as a further 20% reduction in the remaining revenue each year, reflecting market and pricing pressures beyond the initial LOE impact.

What is Merck doing about it? Apart from the pipeline efforts to replace the hole, in terms of Keytruda itself, efforts are ongoing to extend the use to early-stage cancers and use cases less vulnerable to replacement by biosimilars.

Merck is also actively advancing its pipeline across several therapeutic areas. In oncology, the company is developing promising candidates such as Winrevair, the V940 personalized mRNA cancer vaccine in partnership with Moderna, and T-cell engagers through its acquisition of Harpoon Therapeutics. In immunology, Merck has strengthened its position with acquisitions like Prometheus Biosciences, which focuses on inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) and lupus.

The aggressive revenue erosion of ~40% does not factor in the pipeline replacement potential at all, as the P/S ratio (and share prices) have both corrected by a similar ~40% in the past year. I acknowledge that the loss of market-leading products is more damage to valuations than my linear expectation. Also, the market is skeptical about pipeline replacements. In some cases, so am I. Recently, I have had my concerns about the operational challenges to get to Moderna cancer treatment's commercialization. However, that concern is from a 2-3 year window view. You look at Keytruda impacts starting 2028 and three years out from there, and long-term prospects improve as there is enough time to work out the hurdles.

But more in defense of my viewpoint, no pipeline replacement and aggressive revenue losses (unlikely) should at worst balance out that premium valuation erosion. In short, valuations are supportive. The extent of support is extremely good in my view. If there are counterarguments to that, even then, the bottom is not far from current levels.

The Gardasil Conundrum

It would have been great if all the fall in MRK could be attributed to the Keytruda issue. Obviously, that is not the case. Markets are bearish because of other impending short-term concerns as well. The second leading offering from Merck, Gardasil, has been facing demand headwinds in China. In Q1 2025, sales were reported at $193m vs $1.3b in Q1 2024. Further shipments to China have been temporarily halted to stabilize the supply.

I believe the China sales are low enough to assume the bottom. Despite the lows, the drop in overall Gardasil sales was a more manageable 40% YoY, supported by positive 14% growth ex-China (demand in Japan, US, and pricing improvements). If the growth ex-China is retained and even if China does not recover, we could be looking at overall Gardasil growth from here on. I am even discounting the bounce-back potential in China from the approval for use in males.

Revenue and Margin Impacts

Revenue did see a marginally negative YoY change in Q1 2025, but there is not much room for further significant revenue downsides in China. There is, admittedly, no major short-term catalyst despite a promising long-term pipeline. Nothing exciting may be expected in the upcoming quarters, but the buy thesis hinges on the long-term optionality with limited downside from here, not short-term triggers.

In fact, gross margins in Q1 2025 had a good story to tell. A favorable product mix (with falling Gardasil sales, Keytruda improved the product mix in favor of higher margins) and a decrease noted in operating expenses, meant margin improvements without a noticeable dent in the top line.

Management guidance is encouraging from the fact that revenue targets have not been cut. Gross margin rates for the full year have been revised downwards from 82.5% to 82% considering tariff impacts of $200m. In fact, even if the tariff uncertainty ends up adverse to Merck's assumptions, which is far lower an impact than I had anticipated, the margins are not expected to be impacted materially. Merck is also looking at low China exposure anyway at this point. There cannot be major shocks due to adverse tariffs for Merck at this point.

Long-Term Growth Drivers

Merck does have a lot of immediate headwinds, including pricing pressure from the US government for regulated pharma prices. But I think a large part of the underperformance for MRK was due to cyclical rotation into AI-led tech rallies, rotating money out of defensives. An upcoming macro uncertainty regime could pull money back into undervalued defensives with good income opportunities.

More importantly, the long-term optionality for Merck is compelling. To diversify beyond oncology, the company has completed targeted acquisitions, enabling a foray into new therapeutic areas such as cardiometabolic and immunology. Its oncology pipeline remains robust, with assets including TIGIT and LAG-3 inhibitors, as well as antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs). In vaccines, Merck is advancing candidates for RSV and CMV, although near-term growth has been weighed down by a sharp drop in Gardasil sales in China. The company is also leveraging AI to accelerate drug development and improve success rates. Meanwhile, geographic expansion and rising healthcare demand in emerging markets support its long-term growth outlook. Even in the short term, despite all the concerns around it, Merck continues to post growth from its Keytruda franchise.

Buy Merck

While headwinds from China sales, impending LOE, and risks from competition, pipeline execution, and regulatory and pricing pressures make strong cases against MRK, the correction from the top makes for a GARP-style entry at these levels. The healthy free cash flow and time in hand will not only enable flexibility to acquire strategic partners but support a very healthy dividend yield of 4.16%.

Merck is a clear Buy at current valuations for long term, GARP, and dividend-focused investors, balancing between short-term support from its defensive positioning and long-term optionality.

data.title: AMD: Valuation Is Attractive Now, Shifting To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) is a leading U.S. fabless semiconductor chip stock. I wrote about the stock last year at a time when the stock was trading at a mid-40s forward P/E range, a multiple I thought too pricey. Since I issued a sell recommendation the stock has fallen steadily with the share price down over 50% in early April from the time of my previous report.

I am switching to a buy recommendation today on the back of a few factors. I will go into detail on these below but in essence what we have seen is a rapidly falling share price, earnings forecasts holding up favorably, new opportunities in the AI space emerging, continued CPU market weakness from Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) and a starting valuation entry point which is no longer excessive.

Taking these factors together, I think now is the right moment to close my sell call on AMD and switch to a buy from this point. I feel the stock looks a compelling buy at current levels, the current valuation has only been available to investors a handful of times in the past decade and arguably the forward opportunity set today offers the most upside in a decade.

Valuation

First despite some downgrades to EPS forecasts, the stock price has fallen far faster than expected earnings. As a result AMD is now trading at a discount to both its five and ten-year average forward P/E. More strikingly is the change in the PEG ratio, when I wrote about AMD last July the stock was trading on a punchy PEG of around 6x. Today investors can pick up shares for a PEG just over 1x which is the cheapest the stock has been in years.

What makes me even more bullish on this valuation is the current consensus EPS forecasts have not adjusted yet for the EPS upside associated with AMD's newly announced Middle East deals, most notably the $10B joint venture with the Saudi HUMAIN national AI company. I see current EPS forecasts as likely underestimating AMD's earnings power, which in turn means the already highly attractive entry valuation would look even more so if EPS forecasts were fully up-to-date.

Valuation is always a bread and butter element of investing for me. The greatest company in the world can be a bad investment if the valuation is too expensive. AMD has swung significantly over the past year from being too expensive for my blood, to looking very attractive today. The attractive entry point on offer for investors is compelling, I think AMD looks the best value in quite a few years.

New Opportunities

The initial phase of AI development was very much concentrated in the data centers of a select few hyperscale cloud players. Media reports suggested the market for AI GPUs was completely dominated by Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA). However, as the market has expanded both within the hyperscale players and beyond, there has been an appetite to avoid a complete reliance on a single vendor. As a result, a mixture of customer silicon, ASICs offered by the likes of Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) and alternative GPU solutions such as AMD have gained traction. The acquisition of ZT Systems, which closed in March, enhances AMD's ability to compete with peers offering full rack-scale solutions for data center compute.

In their most recent quarter AMD reported growth in their data center segment of 57%, delivering revenue of $3.7B. While they omit a breakout of the exact split between data center CPU and GPU revenue, the numbers show a clear indication of demand. Product cadence remains furious with the next iteration of the MI-series of GPUs due for bulk production in mid-2025. Even though AMD is likely to remain a distant second to Nvidia in the data center GPU, the firm still has plenty of room to achieve high growth, especially with the ecosystem of customers expanding.

Last week we got the significant news of an AI partnership between a host of leading U.S. companies and Middle Eastern countries, in particular Saudi Arabia. As part of the slew of announcements, we saw AMD CEO Lisa Su as part of the U.S. delegation in Riyadh. AMD for their part signed a $10B joint venture agreement with the newly formed Saudi national AI company called Humain. This type of deal invariably will involve the supply of chips and software initially, but as we know the pace of hardware development is furious, so client countries will seek to upgrade their chips in a regular cadence, much as a data center cloud company does.

The deal, while momentous in itself, potentially presages a new wave of AI demand from rich sovereign nations looking to own and operate AI infrastructure on their own soil. Given the options countries have are essentially the U.S. or Chinese chip companies, I think its clear that U.S. companies with their tech expertise are well positioned to capture this demand. I could easily foresee a situation where industrialized countries such as Japan would seek to strike similar deals to the Saudis.

PC CPUs are an area where AMD has seen substantial market share gains in recent years. The segment is a bit boring compared to data center GPU, but it does tell a story of solid execution by AMD. The ten years ago the firm had a market share of about 9%, in a market completely dominated by Intel. Today that share stands just shy of 30%, an incredible 3x improvement in just ten years. Perhaps the grinding of market share gains over time in CPU could serve as a template for the company to go after Nvidia's GPU dominance. I expect AMD to continue their good run and gain incremental share in CPU as more and more customers find better value in AMD vs their legacy preference for Intel.

Risks

AMD's steady progress in both server and client/PC CPU has at least in part been a function of weakness at Intel. The traditional CPU leader has struggled with its operations and this culminated with the departure of CEO Pat Gelsinger at the end of 2024. A renewed management team at Intel could potentially lead to a turnaround of operations there and a better execution in the CPU market. This does not necessarily mean AMD would cede hard-won share, but it could make gaining further market share incrementally more difficult, especially given Intel's traditional brand advantage, especially with enterprise customers.

AMD, like other tech hardware players, is a cyclical business, the demand for the firm's products naturally waxes and wanes with the economic cycle. The important thing, of course, is the firm's ability to growth earnings consistently through an economic cycle, such that the next peak is higher than the prior and the next trough higher than the previous. If we enter a recession, it is unlikely AMD will have anywhere to hide. Cyclical stocks tend to underperform the market in a downturn as their earnings exhibit more volatility than a healthcare stock, for instance.

We are potentially reaching a point of peak capex by the large hyperscale cloud players, a trend which cannot continue forever. The introduction of sovereign wealth money such as the Saudis helps to de-risk the potential demand destruction in a recession and AMD's valuation is no longer scary expensive, so we have some support in that respect. I like AMD as an investment from this point, but I advise readers the stock will likely be volatile, as evidenced by its five-year beta of close to 2.

Conclusion

Having held a sell recommendation on AMD for almost a year, I am switching my recommendation to a Buy given an evolved picture. AMD is no longer trading at a lofty valuation, especially when the level of growth forecast is considered. The firm's acquisition of ZT systems sets it up to better compete with peers like Nvidia in offering full rack offerings to data center customers.

The AI universe of opportunities has expanded significantly in the past year, with new market entrants such as Middle Eastern sovereign AI players expanding the pie for all chip players. The presence of Lisa Su in Riyadh as part of the U.S. delegation was a clear indication that AMD is well positioned to avail of the next leg of AI spending, given where the stock is trading today, I like the setup from here.

data.title: PGIM Jennison Emerging Markets Equity Opportunities Fund Q1 2025 Commentary

MARKET REVIEW

While the U.S. equity markets were weak in the first quarter, emerging market equities demonstrated resilience, with the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (the Index) gaining 2.9%. A weakening dollar and an investor shift away from the U.S. amid trade policies and concern about looming tariffs were tailwinds.

In the Index, Information Technology was the only negative sector, while Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services advanced double digits.

With respect to the Index’s larger country weights, China, Brazil, and Korea outperformed, while Taiwan, Indonesia, and India declined.

The PGIM Jennison Emerging Markets Equity Opportunities Fund (MUTF:PDEZX) declined slightly in the quarter, returning -1.09%, underperforming the Index, as growth stocks collectively underperformed value stocks in the quarter.

SECTOR PERFORMANCE

The MSCI Emerging Markets Index underperformed the MSCI All-Country World Index (ACWI) but outperformed the MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. during the quarter.

Within the Index, Information Technology was the only negative sector.

Consumer Discretionary, Communication Services and Materials gained the most.

The PGIM Jennison Emerging Markets Equity Opportunities Fund returned -1.09% for the quarter and underperformed the Index.

Communication Services holdings added value in the quarter, driven by strong gains in Kanzhun (BZ). The Chinese on-line recruitment platform that utilizes artificial intelligence to match job seekers to recruiters benefited from better-than-expected recruitment demand.

Consumer Discretionary was another area of strength.

  • BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) reported robust earnings growth for 2024 driven by auto and mobile parts and assembly revenues and offshore expansion.
  • Laopu Gold, a luxury jewelry company in China, is enjoying strong sales momentum and higher store productivity.
  • Declines in India’s MakeMyTrip (MMYT) offset some of the gains. Shares were down without a meaningful catalyst. It continues to benefit from a dominant market share and industry tailwinds.

Industrials detracted in aggregate, but with mixed results for individual holdings.

  • Shares of Brazil’s Embraer (ERJ), the world’s third largest plane maker, rose following reports that it delivered 75 aircraft in the fourth quarter, up 27% from the third quarter. For all of 2024, there were 206 aircraft delivered, representing a 14% increase from 2023.
  • Transformers and Rectifiers manufactures transformers to help India increase its power supply by offering more advanced power solutions. Shares were down perhaps due to profit taking and concerns about the overall industry. We eliminated the position.

Varun Beverages drove underperformance in Consumer Staples due to intensifying competitive pressures, and the position was sold from the Fund.

Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL) hurt results in Financials. Argentina’s largest bank is executing well with discipline on the efficiency side, despite restructuring charges affecting its operating expenses in 4Q24.

The Information Technology sector modestly underperformed.

  • An underweight in Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) was beneficial as it was hurt by several headwinds—the January earthquake in Taiwan, U.S. tariffs, and potential for disruption from DEEPSEEK. The team’s long-term thesis remains intact.
  • Another Taiwanese company, Wiwynn declined in tandem with a sell-off of AI infrastructure companies. We eliminated the position due to its exposure to AI servers.

Portfolio Positioning

During the past quarter, the team made several key allocation shifts at both the country and sector level in response to evolving macro conditions and relative valuations. It is worth noting that outside of some semiconductor exposure, there has been very little exposure to trade or tariffs.

The Fund’s China exposure nearly doubled this quarter, driven by growing confidence in the government’s pro-growth policy pivot that began in late 2024. Recent concrete measures were enacted—including increased fiscal stimulus, targeted support for AI and technology innovation, and a more accommodative monetary stance. These signal a shift away from the restrictive posture of the past two years. We also believe the release of DeepSeek may revitalize technology investments and lead to an earnings upcycle in Chinese internet and technology. The team selectively added to names in industrial automation and next-gen platforms that are trading at attractive multiples with strong medium-term earnings potential.

The team also increased our allocation to Brazil, reflecting an improving macro picture—falling inflation, stabilizing interest rates, and positive real rates—alongside idiosyncratic opportunities in Financials and Industrials.

On the other hand, we significantly reduced the Fund’s India exposure. While the structural growth story remains intact, elevated valuations relative to current earnings potential prompted U.S. to take profits and rotate into better risk/reward opportunities. We’ll look to re-enter at more attractive entry points.

We decreased the Fund’s position in Taiwan, with a focus on reducing exposure to semiconductor and AI supply chain names.

At the sector level, we increased our weight in Communication Services, where we see improving fundamentals, lower regulatory risk, and valuation upside. This was funded in part by trimming Information Technology exposure—exiting names correlated to AI infrastructure that had come under pressure related to the release of the Deep Seek model in January.

Outlook

As of the end of the first quarter, we continue to see a favorable earnings outlook across emerging markets and believe the corrections seen out of developed markets, particularly the U.S., could be beneficial for the asset class.

An accommodative or easing monetary policy globally as well as a softening U.S. dollar should also be tailwinds, while emerging markets’ overall valuations remain very attractive on both an absolute and relative basis.

After several years of underperformance and economic strain, China appears well-positioned to outperform given a government shift toward pro-growth; however, as always, we are focused on idiosyncratic ideas relative to the Fund’s opportunity set.

Despite several positives, as U.S. tariffs unfold, there will likely be additional volatility and uncertainty, which could have a meaningful impact on global growth and ultimately on emerging markets.

The Fund continues to focus on companies with strong business models that can deliver earnings growth through this period at reasonable valuations.

data.title: Nvidia: A Golden Buying Opportunity Before Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

In my previous thesis I explained why Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) is a deeply undervalued stock, and I remain confident that the stock is a "Strong Buy." By the way, NVDA outperformed the broad equity market since February. As I said, the stock is very attractively valued for a company that is an undisputed leader of the AI revolution, and the management is committed to further fortify its ecosystem. AI momentum keeps accumulating, which is another big bullish factor for Nvidia's long-term success.

Fundamental analysis

Nvidia releases its quarterly earnings next week (May 28th), which has historically been by far the most powerful catalyst for the stock. Reasons for my optimism are on the surface: Nvidia's historically extremely strong earnings surprise record coupled with AI tailwinds gaining even more momentum.

Nvidia is expected to deliver a 66% Q1 revenue growth on a year-over-year basis. The bottom line is also expected to demonstrate growth with a 19% year-over-year increase in the non-GAAP EPS. Wall Street analysts are quite bullish on the upcoming earnings release since up revisions significantly prevailed over downgrades.

Nvidia’s Data Center business currently accounts for circa 90% of the company's consolidated revenue, remaining the primary engine of growth. High confidence in this segment is backed by continued rapid acceleration of demand from generative AI and large language models.

There were several positive developments over the last few weeks which significantly boosted my optimism regarding Nvidia's ability to deliver strong Q1 coupled with the upbeat guidance. The company continues working on diversifying its geographic revenue mix, recently announcing a partnership with Saudi company Humain, which will deploy more than 18,000 of its latest AI chips.

Elon Musk's recent interview also looks extremely bullish for Nvidia. Tesla's (TSLA) CEO said, "As long as Nvidia makes GPUs better than Tesla, we’ll keep buying from Nvidia." The reasons behind this bold statement look very sound and reasonable, as Musk expects rapid adoption of robotaxi and humanoid robots. Actually, it is not only Elon Musk who is very bullish on humanoid robots, but also Morgan Stanley. According to this highly reputable institution, the humanoids' market has the potential to surpass $5 trillion by 2050. Nvidia is striving to get a first-mover advantage in this potentially explosive market as the company expands its "real-world AI" ecosystem via collaborations with GM, Volvo, Uber, and many other players.

The momentum of generative AI is also accelerating, as BCG suggests that AI/ML is the category that is the most likely to see substantial businesses' IT spending increases. Therefore, it is reasonable that competition in the field continues to intensify, which is beneficial for NVDA as further development of large language models, or LLMs, will require more GPUs. There were two big LLM enhancements rolled out recently, including the new Anthropic generation as well as several Gemini-related improvements from Alphabet.

Another strong factor that speaks volumes about the accelerating momentum in AI chips demand is the recent announcement that Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) raised prices on its most advanced manufacturing nodes. This is a bullish indicator for the entire industry, because the ability to exercise pricing power means that the demand is soaring. I have never seen in my life that a company is able to demonstrate pricing power in an industry that is cooling down or demonstrates limited upside.

Valuation analysis

The forward P/E ratio setup looks absolutely bullish with the metric expected to halve between FY2025 and FY2028. Such a low forward P/E ratio is certainly unfair considering NVDA's dominant positioning in the AI revolution.

Using the same discount rate as I used in February looks like a sound approach, because any sharp moves from the Fed in terms of the monetary policy is extremely unlikely. The reasons are on the surface: President Trump's tariffs and high inflation expectations. Therefore, I apply the same 9.1% WACC that I used in my previous NVDA DCF.

I use a 7% constant growth rate to calculate NVDA's terminal value ("TV"). Reasons for such optimism look robust, in my opinion because AI momentum keeps accumulating and NVDA's positioning remains rock-solid. I know that some experts and analysts say that, in theory, it is impossible for a company to outpace the GDP growth over decades. While I respect all experts and the theory, I have extremely reasonable arguments against such an approach. To start with, comparing it to the broader economy's growth is unfair because companies can grow inorganically (M&A), while the GDP of any country can rely only on organic growth. To proceed, Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL) maintain a 22.8% and 17% revenue CAGR since over the last four decades, respectively.

For FY2026-2027, I used revenue growth forecasts from consensus because it is an average opinion of a large sample of Wall Street analysts' calls. For the period between FY2028 and FY2031, I used a 100-basis point per year growth rate deceleration. Nvidia's trailing twelve month FCF margin is 33.86%, which I forecast to expand by a conservative 50 basis points yearly because of Nvidia's historically strong operating leverage. According to SA, there are 24.39 billion shares outstanding.

My target price estimate is close to $228, which indicates a massive 71% upside potential. Wall Street analysts are not that optimistic, with their average target price of $163. It means that they are applying something around 6% as a constant growth rate, which looks ultra-conservative.

To prove that a 6% constant growth rate is extremely conservative for a company like NVDA, let's refer to the history once again. Do not get me wrong, but even companies like McDonald's (MCD) and Coca-Cola (KO) maintained a 5%+ revenue CAGR over the last four decades. I am saying "even" because these two iconic companies are just kind of defensive companies in commoditized industries that never experienced something like AI tailwinds. Therefore, I think that incorporating only a slightly higher constant growth rate for NVDA compared to Coca-Cola and McDonald's is extremely unfair.

To conclude valuation analysis, I believe that a 7% constant growth rate for Nvidia is much fairer than 6%. It means that the stock deserves a $228 target share price and a $5 trillion+ market cap.

Mitigating factors

While the probability of Nvidia delivering a strong quarterly performance next week, there is no guarantee that it will be a dual beat. In addition, even in the case of strong performance against consensus estimates and robust guidance, there is no guarantee that investors will absorb earnings release with optimism. Therefore, there is some possibility that the upcoming earnings release might be a negative catalyst if the market's expectations are substantially higher. A high Fear and Greed Index from CNN suggests that future growth expectations on the broader market's further growth are rather high.

Another indication that the market's expectations might be too hot is NVDA's current 14-day RSI, which is a crucial indicator from the technical analysis perspective. The current level is around 67%, which is almost the highest level so far in 2025. This factor also elevates the risk of the market's weak reaction to the earnings release even if NVDA's performance is extremely strong.

Conclusion

I am absolutely confident that Nvidia is significantly undervalued, which makes the stock a no-brainer "Strong Buy." The management is relentless in driving innovation and fortifying the company's ecosystem of hardware and software, which cements Nvidia's strategic positioning in the AI era.

Welcome to the May 2025 cobalt miners' news.

The past month saw cobalt prices flat.

Cobalt price news

As of May 21, the cobalt spot price was at US$15.28/lb, flat from US$15.28/lb last month. The LME cobalt price is US$32,770/tonne. LME Cobalt inventory is 140 tonnes, no change from last month. More details on cobalt pricing (in particular the more relevant cobalt sulphate), can be found here at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence or Fast Markets MB.

Cobalt market news

On April 24, CNBC reported:

Trump aims to fight China's control of minerals by investing in miners... Burgum said the Trump administration is considering investing in companies that mine and process critical minerals. The administration is also considering a sovereign risk insurance fund to protect corporate investments in approved projects...The U.S. needs to stockpile key critical minerals through a mechanism similar to the strategic petroleum reserve, Burgum said... "Those three things would put us in the game around critical minerals - the stockpiling, the sovereign risk insurance and the ability to take an equity position. We're working on all three of those," he said.

data.title: Mapfre Has Now Become A Solid Income Idea
data.description:
Introduction

Mapfre, S.A. (OTCPK:MPFRF, OTCPK:MPFRY) is a Spanish insurance and reinsurance company and has been working hard on restoring its profitability. The efforts are paying off, as the company generated 29 cents per share in earnings last year, and appears to be on track to generate 35 cents per share in 2025. As the company’s Q1 trading update confirmed the robust performance, I wanted to have another look at Mapfre to see if I should sell my position or add to it.

Mapfre has its primary listing in Madrid, where the average daily volume is almost 3 million shares. The ticker symbol in Madrid is MAP and it is clear that listing offers superior liquidity.

A look back at the very profitable year 2024

Unfortunately Mapfre does not publish detailed financial statements on a quarterly basis, so we will have to wait for the company’s H1 report to get a detailed look under the hood.

That’s why I think it makes sense to take a step back and have a look at the 2024 results as that will provide the necessary context to discuss the Q1 2025 trading update.

As the income statement below shows, Mapfre generated north of 23B EUR in premiums from its normal insurance activities, while its reinsurance division generated an additional 8.4B EUR, resulting in a total of 28.1B EUR after also deducting holding expenses.

This resulted in a net profit of 902M EUR and on an underlying basis, excluding the impact of the impairment charge on the value of the goodwill on the balance sheet, the net profit was even 992M EUR. Divided over the 3.08B shares outstanding this results in an EPS of 0.29 EUR on a reported basis and an even higher EPS of just over 32 eurocents on an underlying basis.

While the majority of the earnings are paid out by Mapfre as a dividend, the insurance and reinsurance company still retains a very substantial portion of its profits. Based on the FY 2024 performance, Mapfre decided to set the full-year dividend at 16 cents per share for a payout ratio of 55% based on the reported earnings.

At the end of 2024, the total equity stood at 8.89B EUR for a book value of just under 2.90 EUR per share. If you’d deduct the 2.54B in intangible assets, the tangible book value of around 6.35B EUR represents approximately 2.06 EUR per share.

The Q1 2025 trading update is encouraging

Mapfre started the current financial year very well. It reported a 5.4% increase in the insurance premiums on a reported basis (and an increase of in excess of 8% at constant exchange rates) while the combined ratio decreased to just over 94%. It is also encouraging to see the reinsurance division reporting a combined ratio of 97.9% despite being impacted by the California wildfires to the tune of 85M EUR. Despite the impact of the wildfires, the reinsurance division contributed 38.3M EUR in net profit in the first quarter of this year.

On the Q1 2025 conference call, the Mapfre management mentioned the insurance company’s reserves are still in the upper end of its confidence interval, and Mapfre is cautious this quarter as it doesn’t want to be caught off-guard by a stormy spring or summer season.

Looking at the summarized results, Mapfre mentioned a net profit of almost 276M EUR in the first quarter of this year, mainly thanks to a very strong result in LatAm and North America where the contribution showed a very strong improvement. And let’s also not discard the 66% profit increase in the Iberia division thanks to a sharp decrease of the combined ratio and realizing almost 19M EUR in financial gains (an increase compared to the less than 11M EUR in Q1 2024).

Looking at the analyst consensus estimates for this year, the consensus appears to call for a net profit of approximately 1.1B EUR, which would result in an EPS of approximately 35 cents per share.

Investment thesis

Mapfre’s share price has performed well in the past few years as the stock is up 79% since my article in Q4 2023 was published. The Spanish insurance and reinsurance company is a robust performer but we have to be mindful of the natural catastrophes the reinsurance division could be exposed to.

I think Mapfre should be seen as an attractive income idea. Assuming the dividend increases from 16 cents to 17 cents, the dividend yield would increase to close to 5% (subject to the standard Spanish dividend withholding tax rate of 19%), while the payout ratio would remain at just around 50% (paving the way for further dividend increases in the next few years). And given the relatively high earnings retention ratio, the book value and tangible book value per share will continue to increase as well.

I currently only have a small long position in Mapfre, as the share price started to run away from me before I could further expand my position. Given the robust performance of the company I don’t really want to sell either, so I’ll just have to bite the bullet and add to my position on a weak day.

data.title: Dell Technologies: Q1 Should Change The Game
data.description:
Introduction

My previous and so far the only Seeking Alpha article on Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL) stock came out in February 2024 with a "Strong Buy" rating. I argued that the stock was setting up for a big upside because of its undervaluation and "multipolar exposure to diverse market dynamics, particularly in the AI and infrastructure sectors." Since then, the total return of DELL was negative 3.3% while the S&P 500 index (SPX, SP500) showed negative 4.57%, so DELL seems to be doing OK relative to the broader market.

Next week, on May 29th, 2025, Dell Technologies is set to report for its fiscal Q1 FY2026, which I believe should become a major trigger for DELL to reverse its dip and continue its reversal rally in the medium term.

Let's Quickly Review DELL's Q4 FY2025

For its fiscal Q4 FY2025, Dell Technologies saw revenue of $23.9 billion (+7% YoY), which was 2.6% lower than the consensus expected initially because of the weakness in the consumer revenue part of their Client Solutions Group (CSG) segment. Anyway, the firm's margins seem to stay in stable territory and even expand, so the Q1 EPS of ~$2.68 managed to beat consensus estimates by about $0.16 or ~6.42%.

What drove up the margins primarily was Dell's record profitability in the Infrastructure Solutions Group (aka ISG) business: For Q4, they reported an 18.1% EBIT margin, which was significantly more compared to the 8% in Q1 FY2025. The AI growth engine at Dell seems to be firing on all cylinders, staying the main growth driver overall for the consolidated results of the firm. During the Q4 earnings call, the management revealed Dell's AI server backlog was ~$4.1 billion, but then it increased up to "roughly $9 billion" as of late February. They shipped ~$2.1 billion in AI servers in Q4 alone, and they're not going to stop there (more on that in the next chapter of this article).

The margin profile of AI servers, while it seems to be dilutive to gross margin, is actually accretive in terms of margin dollars and EBIT, the management noted. Regarding Blackwell margins, Jeff Clarke commented on the call that they were initially lower than Hopper, but there are "numerous levers for improvement through value-added engineering and services, especially as AI solutions scale to the enterprise, where margins are typically better."

I don't see a major headwind for Dell's overall marginality levels in the next few years because the enterprise AI adoption is still in very early innings, or "still on Dell's way to the stadium," as Yvonne McGill put it during the latest Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference.

We have over 2,000 enterprise customers that are at the beginning stages of tests within their companies, but not at scale. Our - what we're still seeing that dominant portion of our revenue is coming from those Tier 2 hyperscalers.

So I think of the demand environment. I think it's very robust. It's expanding. It's expanding both in the Tier 2 hyperscaler level, but it's also more excitingly or equally as exciting in the enterprise, right, where I do feel that we have even more differentiation. Our solution is differentiated and advantaged. But when you get into the broader enterprise, you get to take advantage of all the rest of Dell's capabilities, right, and our scale, our global scale and services and solutions, and it's very exciting opportunity for us.

Source: Morgan Stanley's tech conference [May 5, 2025].

At the same time, traditional server demand remains robust, with 5 consecutive quarters of YoY demand growth by Q4 FY2025 and McGill's commentary regarding "double-digit growth in PowerScale," and "an expanding PowerFlex buyer base."

On the CSG side, while I've already noted the consumer weakness (-12% in sales), the commercial revenue there was up 5% YoY, so the consolidated segment's top line even managed to go up, albeit by just 1%. The drop in EBIT by 19% YoY is something I think is cyclical - I'll touch upon that in the next chapter.

Because CSG growth hasn't materialized as strongly as it was initially anticipated, and also because great investments in AI-related inventory were made in the past few quarters, which impacted the cash conversion cycle, Dell's unlevered FCF was only ~$627 million for full FY2025 compared to almost $7.4 billion in FY2024. But the historical tendency for CCC improvement from current levels suggests that in FY2026, FCF conversion should return to or exceed 100% of net income, boosted by a likely comeback of the CSG segment, which traditionally "prints money" due to its favorable cash conversion.

Speaking of the balance sheet as of Q4, I see $5.2 billion in cash and investments, which is down sequentially, but Dell's core leverage ratio improved sequentially to 1.2x, kind of offsetting the negatives here, in my opinion.

Let's Preview Dell's Q1 FY2026

The past is the past. Dell's Q4 results have already been priced in as they came out back in late February this year - it's time for us to be forward-looking and try to forecast the future (sort of).

First off, I noted previously that Dell shipped ~$2.1 billion in AI servers in Q4 alone. As I promised above, I'm telling you what they think of FY2026. Dell's AI server shipments should total to "at least $15 billion," a figure Yvonne McGill reiterated at that Morgan Stanley's conference, saying that they are not limiting themselves to that number. Those $15 billion are just the starting point.

Another point of strength should come from CSG, which is guided to grow by "low to mid-single digits, weighted towards the second half of the year." I see several pillars for that growth to sustain itself. First, the PC installed base is aging, with "360 million PCs bought in 2021 turning four years old this year." Second, the end of support for Windows 10 in October 2025 should lead to an estimated >500 million PCs running today being unable to run Windows 11. Third, having introduced "the most Copilot+ PCs powered by ARM-based Qualcomm Snapdragon processors" and the "broadest portfolio of Intel Lunar Lake commercial PCs", Dell's market standing in AI PCs is only going to progress and add another layer of top-line growth.

Also, in FY2025, DELL's OPEX was down by 4% YoY, and for FY2026, it is also "expected to be down low single digits" thanks to the management's "Modern Dell" initiative, which is basically about deploying AI internally at the firm to save on some costs and simplify operations.

Regarding specific guidance figures for Q1 FY2026: Dell guided for $23 billion in sales in the midpoint (+3% YoY), and despite the expected OPEX strengthening, the EBIT margin is expected to be down sequentially due to seasonality in ISG. Anyway, the Q1 EPS figure at the midpoint suggests $1.65, which is ~25% above last year's figure. The management also guided that there might be a fluctuation of +/- 10 cents to that internal estimate, and it let the Wall Street professionals covering the stock price in a slight premium to the midpoint (even though the premium is only 4 cents or ~2.4%).

I think the odds are that Dell beats the current estimates again, thanks to the overall PC market's strength. According to IDC data (April 2025), although Dell lost 0.3% of its PC market share in Q1 based on preliminary data, its overall PC shipments have risen by 3.22% YoY.

Also, I expect Dell's AI server backlog to show continuing expansion, which is what might drive up investors' interest to give DELL some valuation premium.

Dell's Valuation Update Before Q1 Print

DELL stock is currently trading at 12x forward earnings, which is a sharp multiple contraction vs the TTM figure of 18x (i.e., the contraction priced in today amounts to ~33%):

At the same time, the Street prices in another few years of multiple contractions amid the continuing EPS expansion, which is going to lead to PEGs below 1x starting in 2026:

I don't think this setup looks sustainable. There are 2 options on how this is going to change: Whether the Street revises the current EPS estimates to the downside, or the DELL stock price should reprice higher. I believe in the latter option.

Analysts at Argus Research (proprietary source, February 2025) agree with me, seeing a massive upside potential of DELL stock in the next 12 months:

Our peer-calculated value for the shares is around $205, reflecting positive company fundamentals and some slowing in the tech sector rally. On a discounted free cash flow basis, we calculate a value of around $260, in a rising trend as AI excitement supersedes PC softness.

In all, our blended valuation for DELL is above $215, in a rising trend. Appreciation to our 12-month target price of $150, along with the 1.6% current yield, implies a total potential return, on a risk-adjusted basis, exceeding our forecast market return and consistent with a BUY rating.

I'm not seeing that much. If the stock's P/E adjusts higher thanks to beatings in Q1/Q2 this year, say, to 15x P/E, and the full-year EPS beat is only 5%, the fair value is ~$145.8/share. That's an upside of 32-33% to the current price.

Risks And Final Thoughts

One of the risks that I see for my "Buy" thesis is Dell's cyclicality dependence on PCs: In case the CSG segment doesn't recover the way expected and Dell misses again on the top line this time, the likely scenario in this case would be a correction to the low 90s (based on my technical analysis).

Also, as I noted in my previous take on DELL, in the current environment where low-margin CSG is doing better than higher-margin ISG, Dell oftentimes gets a pass from investors, which leads to lower valuation multiples. Given that, we might continue to see no multiple expansion for DELL.

Despite all that, the stock's prospects look bright, in my view. The upcoming Q1 report should bring back optimism and help DELL recover from its YoY dip of >24%.

I keep my "Buy" rating in place, waiting for another EPS beat for Q1 FY2026.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Murphy Oil: Potential Elephant Talk
data.description:
Murphy Oil (NYSE:MUR) announced another Vietnam discovery. This is raising hopes of another find of significant oil offshore. Murphy has long been known for slow growth, a strong balance sheet, and a solid exploration program. The result has been that some commercial discoveries have been found over the years. But that elephant that the whole offshore industry "goes for" has yet to be found. This is not that unusual because Exxon Mobil (XOM) for example, explored in Guyana for something like 2 decades before the first discovery was announced. Now, management has a bunch of solid prospects lined up that could again prove to be extremely impactful. Given the history here, the worst that could happen is likely to be more slow growth.

Near Term Strategy

Here are the highlights of what is hoped to be a very impactful next year or two.

Management's take on this from the conference call is:

"Murphy's team will also continue our plan to drill several meaningful international exploration wells over the next 12 months, which will test prospective un-risked resources that equal 5 times our current offshore proved reserves."

This quote is from Eric Hambly that was made as comments to the slide above during the conference call.

The key here is that management is not "betting the company" as so often happens with offshore players. That very often turns the exploration into a "make or break" program that often ends with broken. Instead, this management has been very successful at limiting the downside potential (in the past) while opening up the upside potential for an asymmetric return.

The result of this is a slowly growing business with both a dividend and stock repurchase program as well as a strong balance sheet while shareholders wait for that eventual bonanza everyone in the offshore business hopes to find.

Vietnam

Probably the highlight of the near-term program is the discoveries in Vietnam. Those discoveries lower the immediate risk of more wells because oil has been found.

This discovery came first. There is enough evidence here that management is targeting first oil production near the end of fiscal year 2026.

One of the things mentioned is that these discoveries are thought to be robust enough that they will keep going through a period of weak prices. That likely means very low breakeven points that are typical for the offshore business.

Despite the often-large upfront costs in the offshore business, many operators demand very low breakeven costs before they invest that kind of money. That is the case here.

Note that the net oil pay is about one-third of what it was with the original discovery. However, that well is still considered commercial. More importantly if the geology backs up the map shown above, developing everything in between has a lower risk than before the second discovery.

In summary, this part of the business is looking a whole lot more significant than was the case in the past.

Côte d’Ivoire

This relatively new business has no discoveries made by Murphy. But Murphy has long followed others into an area by choosing acreage that is on trend with known discoveries. Murphy rarely does just plain exploration where there is no production without some sort of risk mitigation.

Murphy also plans to potentially develop the discovery that already exists (as shown above). Note that Murphy is early enough in this play to grab some acreage with good upside potential while the downside risk has clearly been minimized.

Earnings

First quarter earnings were impacted by several unexpected one-time items that materially decreased production. Just getting past those one-time items is going to materially increase second quarter production. On top of that is some growth factored into the budget.

The whole situation will affect full year average production as that is now forecast to come at the low end of the guidance. We still have not gotten to hurricane season which could materially affect production beyond guidance assumptions.

Long-term debt did increase because the budget was reaffirmed. But the debt ratio is well within the comfortable range. Mr. Market seems to get all wound up about debt increases when the capital budget exceeds cash flow but does not take into account the first quarter one-time items. Nor does the market seem to account for a comfortable debt load even if it is higher. That combination of factors may provide a solid point of entry for investors willing to take advantage of a nonrecurring situation.

The fact is that this company is just below investment grade. The first quarter did nothing to change that. The exploration strategy is clearly created to protect the balance sheet. To me, this is the way to explore offshore, and all these worries are likely a buy signal.

Summary

Murphy Oil has a very long history of solid discoveries that net out to benefit shareholders when the dry holes are considered. More importantly, past articles have noted how Murphy has partnered with some outstanding companies like Exxon Mobil (XOM). That puts Murphy at a certain level above a lot of "stars that shine and then burn out".

The time to consider a company like Murphy is likely now when the long-term outlook is particularly good. But the stock price got cut in half because Mr. Market sees a period of weak commodity prices. Therefore, Mr. Market ignores the fact that the offshore business generally proceeds whenever the breakeven price of the project is low. The offshore business has a very long-term horizon. Investors would be wise to consider the same strategy.

Murphy Oil is a strong buy consideration due to the current outlook. If history is any guide, there will continue to be slow growth until that elephant is found. I have long felt that this is a solid way to play the offshore industry because each try does not "bet the company" or in the long-run burden the company with so much debt that it becomes a zombie corporation.

To me, the quarterly reports pale in comparison to the future "homerun" prospects. It took decades for Exxon Mobil to find Guyana. Yet partner Hess (HES) was a bargain for a couple of years after the initial discovery because it took about 7 years for that first well to begin production. The market really did not respond until there was cash flow from the discovery. Now things are speeding up as infrastructure and an industry is established. Murphy often waits for preliminary results before wading into an area. That somewhat reduces time frames.

Risks

The biggest risk is that management does not properly evaluate the risk and a perfectly good track record changes for the worse (for either a period of time or permanently).

The current political uncertainty caused by the tariffs and threats of trade wars threatens the business of American companies overseas. This article is written with the idea that the current situation is posturing for negotiations that replace the current situation with something permanent and constructive.

Last Article

The last article mentioned the new CEO's role in all of these possibilities. Probably the largest risk is in offshore risk assessment on these projects. This CEO needs to establish his own track record to reassure the market that he is up to the job.

There is a start here of negative free cash flow that likely concerned the market. Yet, the previous CEO likely would have made the same decision. You cannot predict nonrecurring items in this business. It is part of the risks of the business.

In short, the new CEO is at least partially getting a "trial by fire". Let us see how this goes.

data.title: Vacation Plans Shrink With Consumer Confidence
data.description:
According to the University of Michigan’s preliminary May survey, the U.S. Consumer Sentiment Index fell to 50.8, down from 52.2 in April. This marked the second-lowest reading in the survey’s nearly 75-year history, surpassed only by June 2022.

The decline is attributed to heightened concerns over inflation and the economic impact of President Trump’s trade policies. Notably, 75% of respondents spontaneously mentioned tariffs, up from 60% in April.

Inflation expectations have surged, with year-ahead projections rising to 7.3%, the highest since 1981, and long-term expectations increasing to 4.6%.

The Conference Board’s Consumer Confidence Index® also reflected a downturn, dropping by 7.9 points in April to 86.0 (1985=100). The Expectations Index, which gauges consumers’ short-term outlook, fell to 54.4, the lowest since October 2011 and well below the 80-point threshold that typically signals a recession

Travel is a discretionary expense that reflects consumer sentiment. When people are less confident about their financial prospects, they are less likely to plan vacations or leisure trips.

According to the Conference Board, the share of Americans planning to take a vacation in the next six months slipped below 40% in February for the first time since the pandemic. It ticked up slightly to just above 40% in April, but that was down from more than 44% a year earlier.

Americans cite economic concerns and stock market weakness as reasons to cut back on summer vacation plans; many are swapping air travel and extravagant holidays for road trips and shorter vacations.

According to a Deloitte report, 41 percent of respondents are taking a trip of three nights or fewer, compared with 37% last year. See, Europe Is Out. Road Trips Are In. Welcome to the Scaled-Back Vacation:

The market’s rebound and the tariff pause didn’t change Ruswick’s mind. “I don’t have faith this is going to stay this way,” the 29-year-old software engineer said.

He feels pinched, now that prices for soda, meat and seemingly everything else are up significantly from a few years ago. His rent is also higher, and homeownership feels out of reach. The family is now planning a road trip for later this summer to South Dakota’s Black Hills.
